examples.title: Chandy-Misra-Haas Deadlock Detection
examples.description: Algorithm for detecting deadlock using probe messages in distributed systems
examples.code:
// Process initiates probe when blocked and has no dependents
PROCEDURE initiate_probe(process_id)
 probe = (initiator=process_id, sender=process_id, blocker=NULL)
 FOR each process that process_id is waiting for
 SEND probe TO that process
 END FOR
END PROCEDURE

// Process receives a probe
PROCEDURE receive_probe(probe)
 IF probe.initiator == MY_PROCESS_ID THEN
 // Probe returned to initiator - DEADLOCK DETECTED
 REPORT deadlock
 initiate_resolution(probe.initiator)
 ELSE IF I am already in probe.path THEN
 // Already processing this probe - ignore
 IGNORE probe
 ELSE
 // Forward probe to my blocker
 FOR each process I am waiting for
 new_probe = (probe.initiator, MY_PROCESS_ID, blocker)
 SEND new_probe TO blocker
 END FOR
 END IF
END PROCEDURE

// Deadlock resolution
PROCEDURE initiate_resolution(victim)
 SEND abort_message TO victim
 victim.releases_all_resources()
 UPDATE wait_for_graph
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: The CMH algorithm uses probe messages that travel along wait-for graph edges. When a process receives its own probe, a cycle is detected indicating deadlock. The initiator then triggers resolution by aborting a process to break the cycle.

examples.title: Centralized Deadlock Detection
examples.description: Coordinator-based deadlock detection in distributed systems
examples.code:
// Coordinator Site Algorithm
COORDINATOR:
 wait_for_graphs = {} // Map of site_id -> local WFG
 
 PROCEDURE receive_local_wfg(site_id, local_graph)
 wait_for_graphs[site_id] = local_graph
 
 IF all_sites_reported THEN
 global_wfg = MERGE_ALL(wait_for_graphs)
 IF detect_cycle(global_wfg) THEN
 cycles = FIND_ALL_CYCLES(global_wfg)
 FOR each cycle IN cycles
 victim = SELECT_VICTIM(cycle)
 SEND abort TO victim.site
 END FOR
 END IF
 CLEAR wait_for_graphs
 END IF
 END PROCEDURE
 
 FUNCTION detect_cycle(graph)
 visited = {}
 FOR each node IN graph.nodes
 IF dfs_has_cycle(node, visited, {}) THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 END IF
 RETURN FALSE
 END FUNCTION

// Local Site Algorithm
SITE:
 PROCEDURE send_local_wfg()
 local_wfg = BUILD_WFG(local_processes)
 SEND (site_id, local_wfg) TO coordinator
 END PROCEDURE
 
 TIMEOUT every T seconds:
 send_local_wfg()
examples.explanation: Centralized approach has one coordinator that collects wait-for graphs from all sites, merges them, and detects cycles. Simple but has single point of failure and can become bottleneck.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) for cycle detection on global graph

examples.title: Wait-For Graph Construction
examples.description: Building and maintaining wait-for graph at each site
examples.code:
// Data structures
PROCESS:
 id
 state: RUNNING | BLOCKED | TERMINATED
 held_resources: SET
 requested_resources: SET
 waiting_for: PROCESS // NULL if not waiting

RESOURCE:
 id
 type
 owner: PROCESS // NULL if free
 wait_queue: LIST[PROCESS]

// Build local wait-for graph
FUNCTION build_local_wfg()
 wfg = new Graph()
 
 FOR each process p IN system.processes
 IF p.state == BLOCKED THEN
 // Process is waiting for resource
 FOR each resource r IN p.requested_resources
 IF r.owner != NULL THEN
 // Add edge: p waits for r's owner
 wfg.add_edge(p.id, r.owner.id)
 END IF
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN wfg
END FUNCTION

// Update WFG on state change
PROCEDURE on_resource_request(process, resource)
 resource.wait_queue.add(process)
 process.requested_resources.add(resource)
 process.state = BLOCKED
 UPDATE_WFG()
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE on_resource_allocation(process, resource)
 resource.owner = process
 resource.wait_queue.remove(process)
 process.held_resources.add(resource)
 process.requested_resources.remove(resource)
 
 IF process.requested_resources.is_empty() THEN
 process.state = RUNNING
 END IF
 UPDATE_WFG()
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This code shows how to construct and maintain a wait-for graph in a distributed system. The graph captures which processes are waiting for resources held by others. Cycles in this graph indicate potential deadlocks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n*r) where n=processes, r=resources per process

examples.title: Banker's Algorithm for Deadlock Avoidance
examples.description: Resource allocation with safety check to avoid deadlocks
examples.code:
// Banker's Algorithm for Distributed Systems
// Each site runs banker for local resources

CONSTANTS:
 n = number of processes
 m = number of resource types

VARIABLES:
 Available[m] // Available resources
 Max[n][m] // Maximum demand of each process
 Allocation[n][m] // Currently allocated resources
 Need[n][m] // Remaining need (Max - Allocation)

FUNCTION safety_check(available, allocation, need)
 work = available // Work vector
 finish[n] = FALSE
 
 // Try to find safe sequence
 REPEAT
 found = FALSE
 FOR i = 0 TO n-1
 IF finish[i] == FALSE AND need[i] <= work THEN
 // Process can complete
 work = work + allocation[i]
 finish[i] = TRUE
 found = TRUE
 END IF
 END FOR
 UNTIL found == FALSE
 
 // Check if all processes can finish
 RETURN (finish contains only TRUE)
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION request_resources(process_id, request[])
 // Step 1: Request <= Need
 IF request > need[process_id] THEN
 RETURN ERROR "Exceeds maximum claim"
 END IF
 
 // Step 2: Request <= Available
 IF request > available THEN
 RETURN WAIT "Process must wait"
 END IF
 
 // Step 3: Pretend to allocate
 available = available - request
 allocation[process_id] = allocation[process_id] + request
 need[process_id] = need[process_id] - request
 
 // Step 4: Check if safe state
 IF safety_check(available, allocation, need) THEN
 RETURN GRANTED
 ELSE
 // Rollback
 available = available + request
 allocation[process_id] = allocation[process_id] - request
 need[process_id] = need[process_id] + request
 RETURN WAIT
 END IF
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Banker's algorithm avoids deadlocks by ensuring the system always remains in a 'safe state' where all processes can complete. Before granting any request, it simulates the allocation and checks if a safe sequence exists.
